coredata.dc:description: Using lignin as a raw material to prepare fluorescent nanomaterials represents a significant pathway toward the high-value utilization of waste biomass. In this study, Ni-doped lignin carbon dots (Ni-LCDs) were rapidly synthesized with a yield of 63.22 % and a quantum yield of 8.25 % using a green and simple hydrothermal method. Exploiting the inner filter effect (IFE), Cr(VI) effectively quenched the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs, while the potent reducing agent ascorbic acid (AA) restored the quenched fluorescence, thus establishing a highly sensitive fluorescence switch sensor platform for the sequential detection of Cr(VI) and AA. Importantly, the integration of a smartphone facilitated the portability of Cr(VI) and AA detection, enabling on-site, in-situ, and real-time monitoring. Ultimately, the developed fluorescence and smartphone-assisted sensing platform was successfully applied to detect Cr(VI) in actual water samples and AA in various fruits. This study not only presents an efficient method for the conversion and utilization of waste lignin but also broadens the application scope of the CDs in the field of smart sensors.

head.ce:title: High-efficient nickel-doped lignin carbon dots as a fluorescent and smartphone-assisted sensing platform for sequential detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using lignin as a raw material to prepare fluorescent nanomaterials represents a significant pathway toward the high-value utilization of waste biomass. In this study, Ni-doped lignin carbon dots (Ni-LCDs) were rapidly synthesized with a yield of 63.22 % and a quantum yield of 8.25 % using a green and simple hydrothermal method. Exploiting the inner filter effect (IFE), Cr(VI) effectively quenched the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs, while the potent reducing agent ascorbic acid (AA) restored the quenched fluorescence, thus establishing a highly sensitive fluorescence switch sensor platform for the sequential detection of Cr(VI) and AA. Importantly, the integration of a smartphone facilitated the portability of Cr(VI) and AA detection, enabling on-site, in-situ, and real-time monitoring. Ultimately, the developed fluorescence and smartphone-assisted sensing platform was successfully applied to detect Cr(VI) in actual water samples and AA in various fruits. This study not only presents an efficient method for the conversion and utilization of waste lignin but also broadens the application scope of the CDs in the field of smart sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin molecules are rich in hydroxy, phenolic, and methoxy groups, making them a class of complex compounds. Since hydroxyl and aromatic groups are key functional groups that determine the performance of polymeric materials, applying the unique aromatic ring structure of lignin to the green preparation of aromatic subunit carbon nanocrystals is one of the best ways to achieve efficient utilization of lignin [1–3]. Lignin is rich in functional groups and aromatic rings, and carbon dots (CDs) derived from lignin have versatile applications across various fields, including anti-counterfeiting printing [4], biological imaging [5], photocatalysis [6], electrocatalysis [7], chemical testing [8], and are also important precursors for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with different luminescent properties [9,10]. Nevertheless, existing lignin-based CDs often exhibit issues such as low fluorescence quantum yield, single color, narrow detection range, and single detection mode. Therefore, developing multi-modal and highly sensitive analysis methods using the CDs efficiently synthesized from lignin is an important pathway for the high-value utilization of lignin, carrying important theoretical research value and practical application significance.

Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is a common heavy metal, primarily originating from industrial water produced during textile and electroplating processes [11]. Cr(VI) primarily exists in the form of CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2− ions, both of which are highly carcinogenic and mutagenic. Prolonged exposure to a certain level of Cr(VI) in the environment can lead to respiratory diseases [12], genetic abnormalities [13], cancer [14], and even death. Ascorbic acid (AA), commonly known as vitamin C, is an essential nutrient for the human body and is widely present in various fruits and vegetables [15]. A deficiency of AA in the human body can lead to a range of diseases, including colds, anemia, arteriosclerosis, scurvy, etc. [16]. Cr(VI) has strong oxidizing properties, while AA has reducing properties [17]. Therefore, they are often analyzed and detected as a pair in the same sensing system [18,32]. Various detection techniques, including spectrophotometry [19], atomic absorption spectrometry [20], chromatography [21], voltammetry [22], and colorimetry [23,24], have been developed for objects detection. However, most existing detection methods are conducted in laboratory settings, which hinder on-site real-time detection. These methods typically involve long detection cycles, cumbersome pretreatment processes, expensive equipment, complex operation, and are not suitable for public use. In contrast, fluorescence probes offer advantages such as portability, simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity, and strong selectivity compared to various existing detection methods. Fluorescence probes have been applied in the analysis and detection of various target substances, such as Cu2+ [25], HSO3 − [26], Mn2+ [10], Sudan I [9], etc. CDs have been widely used in constructing fluorescence sensors due to their advantages of simple synthesis, good water solubility, excellent luminescent properties, and low toxicity [27,28]. Moreover, numerous studies have demonstrated that the CDs can serve as “on-off-on” sensors for the simultaneous detection of Cr(VI) and AA [29,30]. However, these sensors still face challenges such as a narrow linear range, low sensitivity, and poor selectivity. Additionally, fluorescence methods are difficult to achieve portable, visual, and rapid handheld detection, which poses significant obstacles to the widespread use of sensors. Based on this, we aim to efficiently synthesize CDs using lignin and propose a novel method for the sequential detection of Cr(VI) and AA using a combination of a fluorescence-based approach and smartphone assistance, aiming to provide a fast, sensitive, and portable means of detecting Cr(VI) and AA. The method combines the advantages of fluorescence techniques and intelligent sensing, overcoming their individual limitations. This synergy enhances the precision and sensitivity of intelligent sensing, paving the way for novel sensor applications. It is anticipated to have a significant impact on environmental monitoring, food safety, and various other domains.

In this study, Ni-doped lignin-based CDs (Ni-LCDs) were rapidly prepared using lignin as a precursor through a one-step hydrothermal method, with a yield of 63.22 % and a fluorescence quantum yield of 8.25 %. Cr(VI) effectively quenches the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs, while the reducing properties of AA reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III), thus restoring the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system. Based on this, a Ni-LCDs sensor for the sequential detection of Cr(VI) and AA was constructed. Furthermore, based on the significant color change in the sensing system, we established a smartphone-assisted detection method, achieving fast, sensitive, visual, and portable detection of Cr(VI) and AA. Finally, the newly constructed sensing method was applied to the analysis and detection of Cr(VI) in actual water samples and AA in various fruits and fruit juices, yielding satisfactory results. This fully demonstrates that the constructed fluorescence and smartphone-assisted sensor is a novel sensing system with potential application value.

Lignin, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt solution (EDTA-2Na), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical or chromatography grade. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ•cm, obtained from a Milli-Q system (PLUS-E2-10TJ, EPED, Nanjing, China), was utilized throughout the study.

The preparation process of the Ni-LCDs processed as follows: 0.1 g dealkalized lignin, 0.25 g NiCl2·6H2O, 0.108 g OPD, and 10 mL EDTA-2Na solution (0.2 M) were combined in a 100 mL beaker and subjected to ultrasonic treatment. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated to 180 °C, and maintained for 8 h. After the dark green product was cooled to room temperature, it was filtered and centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 10 min. Subsequently, it was further filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed for 4 h using a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100 Da. The solution was then lyophilized to obtain the solid Ni-LCDs powder.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) and a JEOL JSM-7001F scanning electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) spectrum was obtained using the BRUKER D8 ADVANCE A25 instrument (Bruker-AXS, Germany). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with a Zeta Sizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, Worcestershire, United Kingdom). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Burker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra were measured with a Lambda 950 absorption spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, United Kingdom) and a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA), respectively. The FLS 920 Time Correlation Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) System (Edinburgh Instruments, England) was used to record the nanosecond fluorescence lifetime.

The prepared Ni-LCDs were dissolved in ultrapure water (refractive index, η = 1.33) at various concentrations and compared with the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity and optical density (OD) of quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) in 0.10 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33). The quantum yield (Φst) of Ni-LCDs samples was determined. The OD (absorbance) of the solution at 331 nm was measured using a Lambda 950 absorption spectrophotometer. PL spectra with a 331 nm excitation wavelength were recorded using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. The comprehensive PL intensity was calculated as the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range of 360–640 nm. Subsequently, the integral diagram of PL intensity and absorbance was drawn. The Φst of the synthesized Ni-LCDs was calculated according to formula (1):
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ st

/
K st

2
where K represents the gradient of PL intensity relative to the OD integral diagram, and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” denotes the standard (quinine sulfate), and the “x” represents the sample (Ni-LCDs). For these aqueous solutions, ηx/ηst = 1. To minimize the self-absorption effect, the OD was kept below 0.10 at the wavelength of 310 nm in a 10 mm path-length fluorescence cuvette [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) based on the Ni-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ni-LCDs aqueous solutions with a concentration of 1.5 mg/mL were prepared, each containing 16 kinds of different metal ions (Cu2+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Li+, Sr2+, In2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, and Sn2+) and 17 kinds of different anion ions (NO3 −, S2O3 2−, ClO4 −, HPO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, H2PO4 −, I−, NO2 −, F−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, C2O4 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, SCN−, and Cr2O7 2−), all at a concentration of 0.10 M. In addition, solutions containing 16 kinds of different amino acids (l-Proline/l-Pro, l-Alanine/l-Ala, l-Isoleucine/l-Ile, l-Leucine/l-Leu, Glycine/Gly, l-Arginine/l-Arg, l-Glutamate/l-Glu, l-Glutamine/l-Gln, d-Tryptophan/d-Try, l-Cysteine/l-Cys, l-Tryptophan/l-Try, dl-Asparagine/dl-Asp, l-Valine/l-Val, dl-Homocysteine/dl-Hcy, dl-Serine/dl-Ser, and Glutathione/GSH) were prepared at a concentration of 0.01 M. Additionally, 13 kinds of different medicines (thiamine/VB1, morin hydrate/MH, tetracycline/TC, oleanic acid/OA, nicotinic acid/NA, cinnamic aldehyde/CA, D-biotin/VB7, dopamine hydrochloride/DH, taurine, succinic acid/SA, D-sorbitol, folic acid/FA, and penicillamine/PA) and 12 kinds of different pollutants (dioctyl phthalate/DOP, p-toluenesulfonic acid/TsOH, p-fluorophenylsulfonic acid/PFBA, phenyl hydroxide/PhOH, nitrobenzene, dibutyl phthalate/DBP, diisobutyl phthalate/DIBP, diethyl phthalate/DEP, p-nitrosotoluene/PNT, o-fluorobenzoic acid/OFBA, m-chlorobenzoic acid/MCBA, and 4-nitro-m-cresol), were also prepared at a concentration of 0.01 M, respectively. The PL intensity was measured, and the selectivity was assessed by mixing the ion solution with 2.0 mL of the Ni-LCDs. To evaluate the interference of different ions, each 0.1 mL of ion solution was mixed with 2.0 mL of the Ni-LCDs, and then the PL intensity and spectrum were determined. Subsequently, 10 μL of Cr(VI) was added to the mixture, and the PL intensity and spectrum were measured. To establish the linear range and detection limit of Cr(VI), different volumes of Cr(VI) (ranging from 0 to 230 μL) at a concentration of 5.0 mM were mixed with 2.0 mL of the Ni-LCDs, with 2.0 mL of the Ni-LCDs used as a control. The fluorescence spectra and intensities of all samples were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 313 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing system (Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI)) was prepared by adding 7.0 μL Cr(VI) solution (0.1 M) to a 2.0 mL Ni-LCDs solution (1.5 mg/mL). Subsequently, 10 μL of AA solution (0.1 M) was added to the system. The mixture was thoroughly shaken at room temperature, and the fluorescence recovery spectrum was recorded at the excitation/emission wavelengths of 313/333–600 nm. In a similar manner, other reducing agents, including morin hydrate/MH, D-biotin/VB7, adenosine/AMP, oleanic acid/OA, succinic acid/SA, nicotinic acid/NA, thiamine/VB1, D-sorbitol, folic acid/VB9, and vitamin B2/VB2, were added to assess the selectivity of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) sensing system for AA. To evaluate the interference of different reducing agents, 0.1 mL of each reducing agent solution was mixed with a 2.0 mL of the system, and the PL intensities and spectra were measured. Subsequently, 10 μL of AA was added, and their PL intensities and spectra were recorded. To determine the linear range and detection limit of AA, 0.05 M AA solution with various volumes (ranging from 0.0 to 41.0 μL) was added to 2.0 mL sensing system solution, and the fluorescence spectra and intensities of all samples were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 313 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted for Cr(VI) and AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Cr(VI) were introduced into the Ni-LCDs solution to measure PL intensities. The final concentration of Cr(VI) ranged from 0.0 to 600.0 μM. Images of the solution were captured under visible light and ultraviolet (UV) lamp using a smartphone, and the RGB (Red/R, Green/G, and Blue/B) values were extracted using a Color recognition app. The linear relationship between the B/R ratio and Cr(VI) concentration was then calculated and plotted. In order to minimize background influence, we opted to simulate UV and visible light using a 365 nm UV lamp and a white bulb in a darkroom, respectively. Images were captures at fixed angles and directions. Moreover, plotting B/R as the ordinate further diminished background interference.

To create a sensing system (Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI)), 10 μL of Cr(VI) (0.1 M) was added to 2.0 mL of the Ni-LCDs solution (1.5 mg/mL). Subsequently, 0.0–60.0 μL of AA solution (0.05 M) was added and thoroughly mixed at room temperature. Images of the resulting solution were taken using a smartphone under visible light and UV lamp, and the RGB values were determined by a Color recognition app. The linear relationship between the B/R ratio and AA concentration was calculated and depicted.

In order to explore the selectivity of smartphone sensors to Cr(VI) and AA, the color changes of the Ni-LCDs and the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system with various ions and reducing agents under the irradiation of UV and visible light were investigated, respectively. Furthermore, the anti-interference of smartphone sensors to Cr(VI) and AA was studied by recording the color changes of the Ni-LCDs with Cr(VI) and the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system with AA under the irradiation of UV and visible light in the presence of various ions and reducing agents, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The collected tap water, Fen River water (Shanxi, China), and Surface water (Xiaodian District, Taiyuan, China) were processed as follows: The water samples were filtered through filter paper, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 10 min. The supernatant was then collected for further use. All fresh fruits and commercial fruit juices were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China. 50 g of fresh fruit was added into 50 mL of ultrapure water, and then it was ground into a slurry using a blender. 50 mL of commercial fruit juice was diluted into 50 mL of ultrapure water. The slurry and diluted fruit juice was filtered through filter paper, and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 10 min to collect the supernatant for further use. 1.7 mL of different water samples was mixed with 300 μL of the Ni-LCDs solution (10 mg/mL), and then different concentrations of Cr(VI) was added into the above solution to obtain fluorescence spectra and images under ultraviolet and visible light irradiation. The Cr(VI) content and its spiked recovery rate in actual samples was analyzed in detailed. Using the processed fresh fruits and commercial fruit juices as actual samples, the feasibility of detecting AA in the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system was evaluated. 300 μL of the Ni-LCDs solution (10 mg/mL) and 7 μL of Cr(VI) solution (0.1 M) was mixed with 1.7 mL of the processed fruit and juice solution, and then different concentrations of AA were added into the above solution to acquire fluorescence spectra and images under the irradiation of UV and visible light. Finally, the AA content in the real samples and its spiked recovery rate was evaluated in detailed.

Using lignin, OPD, NiCl2·6H2O, and EDTA-2Na as the main raw materials, the Ni-LCDs was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 8 h, yielding approximately 63.22 %. This yield surpasses that of other reported CDs derived from lignin (Table S1) [31–33]. TEM analysis (Fig. 1A) reveals that the Ni-LCDs were uniformly distributed on the copper grid with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm. The diameter of the Ni-LCDs ranged from 1.20 to 3.42 nm, with an average diameter of 2.32 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The SEM image in Fig. S1A illustrates the morphology of the Ni-LCDs, revealing that dried Ni-LCDs display an irregular agglomeration pattern, consistent with findings reported in previous studied on CDs [34]. The PXRD of Fig. S1B shows that the strongest peak is around 15o, and the carbon crystal size is smaller than that of the graphitic carbon (002) diffraction peak, possibly due to the ordered reduction of the sp2 layers caused by the highly defective carbon structure [35]. The zeta potential was measured at −9.15 mV (Fig. S1C), indicating that the Ni-LCDs carried a negative charge.

The chemical structure and surface functional groups of the Ni-LCDs were determined through element analysis, FTIR, and XPS. The results of integral area and rate sensitivity factors (RSF) indicate that the contents of C, N, O, and Ni in the Ni-LCDs are 39.13 %, 22.48 %, 24.82 %, and 13.57 %, respectively. FTIR analysis results are presented in Fig. 2A, where peaks in the range of 3400 to 3500 cm−1 corresponds to -OH stretching, while the distinctive peaks at 2952 cm−1 is associated with CH stretching. A sharp peak in the region of 1609 cm−1 signifies CO stretching, and a peak at 1396 cm−1 is attributed to CN bending. Other vibrational peaks, including CO (1220 cm−1), -OH/N-H (747 cm−1), and CCl (611 cm−1), can be found in the FTIR spectrum [36–38]. The XPS survey scan (Fig. 2B) reveals four binding energy peaks at 851.3, 528.3, 397.6, and 281.4 eV, corresponding to Ni 2p, O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2C) displays five peaks at 284.9, 286.1, 287.0, 288.3, and 289.0 eV, which are associated with CO, C-O-C, C-Cl/C-OH, CO, and COOH, respectively [30,38,39]. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), the peaks at 531.1, 531.7, 532.5, and 533.5 eV are indicative of CO, C-O-C, CO, and -OH, respectively [40,41]. The four peaks at 399.2, 400.0, 401.5, and 407.1 eV in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) are amino N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and nitrate N, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, there are three prominent Ni 2p peaks at 855.6, 861.2, and 864.7 eV, all indicating Ni 2p3/2 [42]. These results indicate that the surface of the Ni-LCDs is rich in functional groups, ensuring their excellent hydrophilicity and stability.

As depicted in Fig. 3A, a prominent absorption peak at 271 nm (black line), is observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Ni-LCDs, attributing to the π-π⁎ transition of aromatic sp2 domains within the carbon core. Additionally, an absorption peak stemming from the n-π⁎ transition within the CO band is detected at 321 nm, as reported in previous studies [43,44]. The optimal excitation wavelength (blue line) and the emission wavelength (red line) are determined to be 313 nm and 371 nm, respectively. As displayed in the inset in Fig. 3A, the Ni-LCDs solution exhibits vibrant blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. Furthermore, an investigation into the fluorescence patterns of the Ni-LCDs under various excitation wavelengths is presented in Fig. 3B. The emission peak exhibits a redshift from 371 nm to 456 nm as the excitation wavelength varies from 300 nm to 420 nm. This excitation-dependent emission behavior of the Ni-LCDs can be attributed to the π⁎ → n transition of the surface-attached functionalities, as previously reported [30]. Compared to quinine sulfate (Fig. S2A), the fluorescence quantum yield of the Ni-LCDs is calculated to be 8.25 % (Fig. S2B), whereas that of lignin carbon dots (LCDs) without nickel doping is only 2.32 % (Fig. S2C). This suggests that the introduction of nickel significantly improves the luminescent performance of the CDs [42]. In Fig. S3, the PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs is presented under various conditions, including pH, KCl, H2O2, UV irradiation, and xenon lamp irradiation. The PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs remains consistently high, not falling below 80 %, as the KCl concentration increases (Fig. S3A). The influence of pH is depicted in Fig. S3B, showing negligible changes in the PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs within the pH range of 3.0 to 12.0. Under the presence of 4.0 M H2O2, the PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs exceeds 80 % (Fig. S3C), indicating excellent antioxidant stability of the Ni-LCDs. Furthermore, the PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs exhibits minimal changes even after continuous UV exposure for 90 min (Fig. S3D) and xenon lamp irradiation for 90 min (Fig. S3E), highlighting the high photostability of the Ni-LCDs. These results indicate that the Ni-LCDs have the characteristics of hydrophilicity and optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) sensing system for AA determination
ce:section.ce:para: Another significant finding in this study is that Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) can be utilized to establish a novel sensing system for the detection of AA. This is mainly attributed to the fact that AA can restore the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs quenched by Cr(VI). Based on the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, a sensor for the detecting AA is established. The selectivity of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system for AA was initially tested. When different reducing agents are added to the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, only AA can restore the system's fluorescence, while other reducing agents do not exhibit a significant fluorescence enhancement effect (Fig. S4C). This indicates that the sensor exhibits good selectivity for AA. When other reducing agents are present in the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, the addition of AA still restores the fluorescence of the system (Fig. S4C), demonstrating excellent anti-interference performance when the system is employed for AA detection. Furthermore, a detailed investigation was conducted on the sensitivity of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system to the detection of AA. As shown in Fig. S5B, when the same concentration of AA is added to Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) systems with different concentrations of Cr(VI), the fluorescence of the system is consistently restored. The highest fluorescence recovery was observed when the concentration of Cr(VI) was 303.42 μM. Therefore, the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system with a Cr(VI) concentration of 303.42 μM was employed for fluorescence titration experiment. With increasing concentrations of AA, the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system gradually recovers (Fig. 5A), reaching a fluorescence recovery rate of approximately 74.2 % when the AA concentration reaches 790 μM. Within the range of 380 to 730 μM, the AA concentration exhibits a well-defined linear relationship with (FF0)/F0 (Fig. 5B and C), where F and F0 represent the PL intensity of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system with and without AA, respectively. The corresponding linear equation, R2, and limit of detection (LOD) are presented as 20–230 μM and 380–730 μM, 0.9941 and 0.9946, and 0.77 μM, respectively. Finally, this method is compared with other CDs-based methods for detecting AA [28,49–52], indicating that the constructed sensor is comparable and superior to other sensors (Table 2 ). In other words, the constructed sensor exhibits greater sensitivity and a lower LOD for AA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of Cr(VI) and AA based on the Ni-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the mechanism of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence quenching of the Ni-LCDs, a detailed evaluation of the fluorescence lifetimes and zeta potentials of the Ni-LCDs before and after the addition of Cr(VI) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 6A and Table S2, the average fluorescence lifetime of the Ni-LCDs is 6.18 ns. When 100, 200, and 300 μM of Cr(VI) are added to the Ni-LCDs, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the Ni-LCDs are 6.27, 6.21, and 6.20 ns, respectively. Generally, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and surface energy transfer (SET) are typically accompanied by energy or electron transfer, resulting in a shortened fluorescence lifetime of CDs [53]. The addition of Cr(VI) does not alter the fluorescence lifetime of the Ni-LCDs, implying that the fluorescence quenching of the Ni-LCDs is not related to the above mechanisms. During dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs undergoes significant changes, while the absorption spectrum remains unchanged; conversely, static quenching exhibits the opposite behavior [54]. Following the introduction of Cr(VI), the zeta potential of the Ni-LCDs shifts from −9.15 mV to −2.94 mV (Fig. S1C), signaling a robust interaction between the Ni-LCDs and Cr(VI), leading to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex at 350 nm (Fig. 6C), thus inducing fluorescence quenching. Hence, we deduce that the fluorescence quenching of the Ni-LCDs by Cr(VI) may involve static quenching. When the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of the fluorophore, it suggests the presence of an internal filtration effect (IFE) between them [55]. As depicted in Fig. 6C, the absorption peak of Cr(VI) substantially overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of the Ni-LCDs, indicating that fluorescence quenching could be attributed to IFE. Consequently, the fluorescence quenching of the Ni-LCDs by Cr(VI) is predominantly ascribed to IFE and static quenching.

As shown in Fig. 6B and Table S2, when different concentrations of AA are added to the Ni-LCDs-Cr (VI) system, the average lifetime of the Ni-LCDs is between 6.13 ns and 6.37 ns, indicating that the fluorescence lifetime of the Ni-LCDs has undergone minimal changes. As depicted in Fig. S1C, upon the addition of AA, the zeta potential of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system changes from −2.94 mV to 3.77 mV, providing evidence that there is an interaction between Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) and AA. From the UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 6C), it is evident that upon adding AA to the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, the absorption peak of Cr(VI) at 350 nm significantly weakens. This could be attributed to the reduction of Cr(VI) by AA, leading to a reduction in the IFE between Cr(VI) and Ni-LCDs, thereby restoring the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs. The addition of Cr(VI) and AA causes the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs to switch from “ON” to “OFF” and then back to “ON”, indicating that the Ni-LCDs can sequentially detect Cr(VI) and AA (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) and AA detection platform based on the smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: An even more significant discovery is that, with the use of smartphones as an auxiliary tool, it is possible to create a smart sensor for rapid and portable detection of Cr(VI) and AA. The selective and anti-interference performance of detecting Cr(VI) and AA through smartphone sensors has been studied. Apart from Cr(VI), the addition of other ions does not cause any changes in the B/G values of Ni-LCDs' images under UV and visible light irradiation (Fig. S4D), indicating that the Ni-LCDs exhibit good selectivity for Cr(VI). When Cr(VI) is introduced into a Ni-LCDs solution containing various interfering ions, the B/G values of Ni-LCDs' fluorescence images significantly increase, while the B/G values of visible light images significantly decrease (Fig. S4D), demonstrating that other ions do not interfere with Ni-LCDs' detection of Cr(VI). When other reducing agents, excluding AA, were introduced into the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, the B/G values of the solution's fluorescence and visible light images hardly change (Fig. S4E), indicating that the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system have selectivity for the detection of AA. Upon adding AA to the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system containing other reducing agents, the B/G values of the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI)'s fluorescence images significantly decrease, while the B/G values of visible light images significantly increase (Fig. S4E), proving that other reducing agents do not interfere with the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI)'s detection of AA. The above results indicate that the constructed smartphone sensor can be used for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA, exhibiting high selectivity and good anti-interference performance, making it an excellent smart sensor.

When Cr(VI) (0.0–600 μM) was added to the Ni-LCDs solution, the color of the solution gradually turns yellow under visible light irradiation (Fig. 7A). The B/G (Table S3) exhibits a good linear correlation with the concentration of Cr(VI) (B/G = −0.0006 Cr(VI) + 0.9981, R2 = 0.9982), with a LOD of 39.64 μM. Here, B and G represent the Blue and Green values of the visible light image, respectively. Under UV irradiation, the fluorescence of the Ni-LCDs gradually quenched with the increasing concentration of Cr(VI) (Fig. 7B). The B/G (Table S3) of the fluorescence image shows a good linear correlation with the concentration of Cr(VI), with a linear equation, R2, and LOD of B/G = 0.0028 Cr(VI) + 1.5135, 0.9953, and 29.90 μM, respectively. Here, B and G denote the Blue and Green values of the fluorescence image, respectively. Similarly, after adding AA (0.0–1400 μM) to the Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) system, the color of the solution changed from yellow to colorless under visible light, and the fluorescence of the solution gradually enhanced. Excellent linear relationships between the B/G (Table S3) of the visible light and fluorescence images and the concentration of AA can be found. The linear equations, R2, and LOD are B/G = 0.0002 AA + 0.7865, 0.9972, and 37.05 μM for the visible light image, and B/G = −0.0021 AA + 4.4905, 0.9962, and 26.58 μM for the fluorescence image, respectively. When compared to other reported sensors for detecting Cr(VI) and AA with smartphones (Table S4) [56–61], the as-constructed sensor has a broader linear range and lower LOD.

To validate the feasibility of the constructed sensor in practical sample detection, we applied it to the analysis and detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. Taking tap water, Fen River water, and Surface water as the research objects, the constructed sensor was used to detect the Cr(VI) content in water samples, and spiking recovery experiments were conducted. As shown in Tables 3 and S5, Cr(VI) was not detected in any of the three types of water samples. The spiked recovery rates of Cr(VI) in the three water samples ranges between 90.80 % and 100.76 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 4.94 % (n = 3). This indicates that the constructed sensor, utilizing both fluorescence and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection, can effectively detect Cr(VI) in actual samples and provides satisfactory detection results. Furthermore, orange, kiwi, cherry tomato, grapefruit juice, mango juice, and orange juice were taken as the research objects to validate the feasibility of the constructed sensor in detecting AA in real samples. The real water samples used as the test object is shown in Table 3, the recovery rate of Cr(VI) is 92.04–100.76 %, and the RSD is 0.57–3.10 % (n = 3). Ni-LCDs-Cr(VI) sensing platform was used to determine AA in fresh fruits and commercial fruit juices, as shown in Table 4 . In the above real samples, the recovery rate of AA is 90.76–103.46 %, and the RSD is 0.23–9.78 % (n = 3). We used the smartphone sensing platform to detect Cr(VI) content in real water samples and AA content in fresh fruits and commercial fruit juices, as shown in Tables S5 and S6. The recoveries are 90.80–99.40 % (n = 3) and 92.26–103.57 % (n = 3) with RSDs of 1.34–8.74 % (n = 3) and 0.57–6.53 % (n = 3), respectively. These results confirm the feasibility of the newly constructed Cr(VI) sensor for detecting Cr(VI) and AA. Meanwhile, the smartphone sensing platform developed in this study is suitable for the rapid and portable detection of Cr(VI) and AA.

In summary, this study efficiently prepared Ni-doped lignin-based carbon dots (Ni-LCDs) using lignin as the precursor. The Ni-LCDs not only exhibits excellent fluorescence performance but also demonstrates fluorescence quenching in the presence of Cr(VI) and subsequent recovery in the presence of AA, making it a rare and high-performance sensor. The reversible fluorescence response allows for easy switching between the “ON” and “OFF” states, enabling rapid detection of Cr(VI) and AA. More importantly, the smartphone sensor based on the Ni-LCDs achieved sensitive, interference-free, and highly selective detection of both Cr(VI) and AA. Finally, the fluorescence and smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor were applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples, as well as AA in fruits and fruit juices, with the detection results remaining free from any interference. This work not only provides a new pathway for the high-value utilization of lignin but also offers new insights into constructing sensors based on the CDs. This will catalyze the advancement of portable sensor technology, fostering greater innovation and potential within environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnostics. Concurrently, it offers valuable insights and inspiration for achieving sustainable development and promoting resource recycling.
